```python
>>> from furl import furl
>>> f = furl()
>>> f.args['where'] = '1=1'
>>> f.url
'?where=1%3D1'
```

Upgrade furl with

```
pip install furl --upgrade
```

- SPTK: a=0, A=0.35, Python: alpha=0.35
- SPTK: a=0.35, A=0, Python: alpha=-0.35

Are you certain that is a monospace font. `brokenBold` is there to check whether the browser makes monospace characters "bigger" when boldened, i.e. they push other elements aside more. Obviously for a terminal to work, characters need to be aligned properly. I don't think that's what is happening here though if brokenBold is true (and I don't see any bold text there).

It should be easier to replace the outer subshell, making the python script call the fixed command. An explicit direction sounds good to me. (Explicit > implicit and all that)

I would think that backwards should be the default, because it's the only direction that can be started without already having an explicit cursor.

When chaining repeated chunks backwards, the before_cursor would be passed between chunks, and also by the user if starting with an explicit cursor.

When chaining repeat chunks forwards, the after_cursor would be passed between chunks and also by the user in the top level parameter.

Also, just for clarity, I think it's useful in your examples to include the `limit` parameter, since small limits make testing so much more efficient.

It's not clear to me whether there should be two methods -- a lower-level one that doesn't chain, just stops at limit, and a higher-level one that chains the lower level one until stopped by the user (e.g. with islice) or by exhaustion of the data. 

It's not strictly necessary to expose the lower-level one, as the chaining one can do anything that it can, and would seem to be more useful in most real-world situations. But the chaining one would be a bit more complicated to use for the simplest small-N test cases, so I guess having the low-level one exposed would probably be useful. 

Hmm, how about just having the single `audits` method, but having another `repeat=False` (or `chain=False`?) parameter for the chaining case?

> Once you know for sure which version of Twisted will pick up your changes,
> you may want to consider adding a check on the Twisted version in the
> UNIX_FD marshall/unmarshall code that detects the attempt to send/receive
> multiple FDs in a single message and throws an explicit error stating that
> this isn't supported until Twisted version X.Y.Z (pretty sure
> twisted.version or twisted.__version__ or somesuch provides easy access to
> the version being used).

I'm not sure such an approach will work. Twisted's APIs for sending/receiving UNIX_FDs are somewhat detached from the underlying calls to send/recvmsg and how many UNIX_FDs are carried in each such message. The actual APIs only support one UNIX_FD at a time. The current implementation (once my contribution gets in) sends one UNIX_FD per message but supports receiving more than one UNIX_FD per message (calling the protocol's receivedFileDescriptor as many times as UNIX_FDs received).

(apparently, dbus itself is happy to receive two UNIX_FDs in separate messages and forwarding them in a single message -- this is pretty much the test/example client/server I was exploring)

Having said this, from txdbus' prespective, using Twisted's API, there is no way to control how many UNIX_FDs are sent/received per message. All you know is that for versions < x.y.z (to be determined) Twisted will fail when receiving more than one UNIX_FD in a single message -- since it is on the receiving side of the stack, there is no way txdbus can handle that failure because it happens before txdbus gets to act.

In other words, the only safe way of dealing with this is requiring Twisted >= x.y.z in requirements.txt. Or maybe I'm mistaken and there is something else I'm not seeing clearly. :)

That's exactly what we did with the `verify` parameter to decode() since we would like to eliminate it at a later point. Our behavior with the deprecation of the verify parameter is very intentional and well documented (both in the CHANGELOG and using warnings)

The removal of `verify_expiration` is certainly something we wanted to do (for the same reasons we want to remove `verify`), but the actual removal of that parameter in the last release (instead of proper deprecation) slipped by us when we reviewed the PR.

At this point, I'm not sure I'm behind a knee-jerk restoration of the `verify_expiration` parameter since we already ripped the band-aid off. The only negative consequence is that the very few (probably) users who are using that parameter will run into verification issues if they are trying to decode an expired token. In other words, we're actually being a bit more secure. If things were the other way around and the removal of the parameter actually hurt security, I would not be opposed to bringing it back and making another release.

Unless others have a compelling argument, I'm for leaving it as is but documenting the change instead of making another release and having this weird "now its gone; now its back" thing going on.

@Jajao555 You shouldn't really be using `send_command` for sending configuration commands use `send_config_set` instead:

```python
output = session.send_command('rollback 0')
cfg_commands = [
    'delete system syslog',
    'set snmp trap-group traps targets 10.x.x.x',
]
output += session.send_config_set(cfg_commands)
output += session.commit(and_quit = True)
with open("output_file.txt") as f:
    f.write(output)
```

In your above code, you are overwriting the output variable each time (so it will only have the content from the last command).

After the first command you should do:

```
output = output + session.send_command('delete system syslog')
```

Or

```
output += session.send_command('delete system syslog')
```

The += essentially does the same thing (i.e. adds the new output onto the end of the existing output string).

Its complicated because correct() doesn't seem particularly accurate with contractions.  I don't think the new tokenization will fix many contractions because it separates them as different tokens. If the spelling mistake is at the beginning it should get fixed ("cann't"), if the ' is in the wrong place ("ca'nt") it will probably give a wildly inaccurate correction, and if it is at the end ("can'tt") it probably won't correct it.

Your commit took the old:
```
# regex matches: contraction or word or punctuation or whitespace
tokens = nltk.tokenize.regexp_tokenize(self.raw, "\w*('\w*)+|\w+|[^\w\s]|\s")
```
and replaces it with:
```
# regex matches: word or punctuation or whitespace
tokens = nltk.tokenize.regexp_tokenize(self.raw, "\w+|[^\w\s]|\s")
```
if you test it with 
```
b = "I couuldn't spel good, ca'nt and won'tt!"
```
the old one returns the weird empty set.:
```
>>> tokens = regexp_tokenize(b, "\w*('\w*)+|\w+|[^\w\s]|\s")
>>> tokens
['', '', "'t", '', '', '', '', '', '', "'nt", '', '', '', "'tt", '']
```
The new one returns:
```
>>> tokens = regexp_tokenize(b, "\w+|[^\w\s]|\s")
>>> tokens
['I', ' ', 'couuldn', "'", 't', ' ', 'spel', ' ', 'good', ',', ' ', 'ca', "'", 'nt', ' ', 'and', ' ', 'won', "'", 'tt', '!']
```
So when Correct() runs, you get `"I couldn't spell good, ca'it and won'tt!"` because the contractions are separate tokens.
As I posted above, you could use:
```
>>> tokens = regexp_tokenize(b, "\w+'\w+|\w+|[^\w\s]|\s")
>>> tokens
['I', ' ', "couuldn't", ' ', 'spel', ' ', 'good', ',', ' ', "ca'nt", ' ', 'and', ' ', "won'tt", '!']
```
But, because of the limitations of Correct(), it results in `"I couuldn't spell good, can and wont!"` Which is not better. I guess it should be a known limitation that contractions are even more inaccurate than running correct() in general?

I think it should instead be ProgrammingError:
Exception raised for programming errors, e.g. table not found or already exists, syntax error in the SQL statement, wrong number of parameters specified, etc. It must be a subclass of DatabaseError

OperationalError means: Exception raised for errors that are related to the database's operation and **not necessarily under the control of the programmer**, e.g. an unexpected disconnect occurs, the data source name is not found, a transaction could not be processed, a memory allocation error occurred during processing, etc. It must be a subclass of DatabaseError .

In this particular case programmer has control over which method he calls.

I see that you're new to github and presumably pretty new to coding, so I'll point out some things that you should have done differently.  I hope you'll take this in the constructive manner it's intended.

First, when talking about code, you should never use a screenshot.  Just copy and paste the actual code.  If you use a screenshot, I can't reproduce your results without manually re-entering the code, which makes it annoying for me, and leaves you less likely to get help because developers don't want to deal with that kind of thing.  Github has [nice ways to do this](https://help.github.com/articles/creating-and-highlighting-code-blocks/), as I'll demonstrate below.

Second, if you're trying to suggest that another person's code isn't doing what you expect, you should actually show why that is.  I don't know what your `quaternion_to_euler_angles` function is, how it's defined, or what it does.  Following on with the first point, you should actually provide an executable code snippet.  This is called a "minimal working example" (MWE) or a ["minimal, complete, and verifiable example" (MCVE)](https://stackoverflow.com/help/mcve).  It would be more common to see something helpful like this in a github issue:

```python
import numpy as np
import quaternion as quat

def quaternion_to_euler_angle(q0, q1, q2, q3):
    # whatever you actually did here

q = np.array([ 0.99996885, -0.00562096,  0.00491871,  0.00255047])
print(np.linalg.norm(q))
print(quaternion_to_euler_angle(q[0], q[1], q[2], q[3]))
print(quat.as_euler_angles(quat.as_quat_array(q)))
```

And then you should show the results, again, by copying and pasting.

Third, you shouldn't claim that somebody's code is wrong just because it's not doing what *you* expected.  Again, this makes developers less likely to help you, and more likely to just grumble that you haven't tried very hard, then just close the issue.  If you have an argument for why what you're expecting is actually *correct* in some real sense, then you should try to provide that argument and not just blame someone else's code.  But first, you should try to understand their code, which brings us to...

Fourth, you should read the documentation.  I see that you're using ipython.  You can just run `quat.as_euler_angles?` (with the question mark), and it will tell you what this function does.  It's evidently not what you think.

Fifth, if you have a reference that you use to claim that there's a right way to do things (like Wikipedia's terrible article), you should actually read it.  The article points out that it uses "the JPL quaternion convention", which is not used by this package.  (It's also not actually well defined, and extremely uncommon in any case.)  The article also describes its rotations as "the **Body 3-2-1** sequence".  These should indicate to you that the article has made certain choices.  They are not the same choices I have made.  (See my fourth point above.)

Sixth, before you open an issue, you should search the issues (not just the open ones) for similar issues.  You might find that an error in code has been fixed already, and you just need to update your version.  Or you might find other people who have found a workaround that you could use.  Or you might find that people have actually brought this sort of thing up before, and it turns out that it's not a mistake at all.  For example, if you [search through the this repo's issues for Euler angles](https://github.com/moble/quaternion/issues?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=is%3Aissue+euler+angles), you would find two issues that made similar complaints [here](#67) and [here](#71).  You could then read my responses to those and find that there are numerous ways to interpret Euler angles.  I have chosen one and documented it clearly.

---

So putting that all together, you should by now see the resolution to your problem.  No, the results you got were not wrong; they were just different from what you expected.  If you want to use different conventions for Euler angles, feel free to copy my function and adjust it to suit your needs.  But of course, you should really try to avoid Euler angles.

Moved the netlink port scan logic from `NetlinkSocket` to `IPDB`.

If you are interested, whether it becomes engaged during the `IPDB` init, you can turn on the debug logging:

```
import logging
from pyroute2 import IPDB

root = logging.getLogger()
root.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)


def get_routes():
    with IPDB() as ipdb:
        routes = ipdb.routes
        result = {
            dst: routes[dst].dump()
            for dst in routes.keys()
        }

for i in range(10):
    get_routes()
    print('{}: OK'.format(i))
```

The issue description:

* Kernel < 4.0 allows multiple `bind()` call until the first success
* Kernel >= 4.0 doesn't — the first failed `bind()` makes the socket useless

This excludes the netlink «port» scan functionality from the socket level. So if the kernel doesn't support the netlink «port» reuse, this scan can be done only by creating new socket objects.

With the issue you reported I already managed to fix a netlink port leakage that existed in `NetlinkSocket`, thanks.

Please, check if the solution works for you. And thanks again for the report.

@v-chojas Is this something you can confirm with someone on the SQL Server driver team?

I think the explanation of the difference is correct, but I'm not sure which piece of code has a bug.

The MS docs on [ColumnSize](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/odbc/reference/appendixes/column-size?view=sql-server-2017) are pretty clear that ColumnSize should be the number of characters, which I would have expected to be unrelated to encoding.

A one-character string with a [half-moon](http://www.fileformat.info/info/unicode/char/1f31c/index.htm) is 4 bytes long when encoded in UTF-8 or in UTF-16.  However, the number of *code points* is certainly still one.  I would have assumed that "characters" meant code points.

Are we sure this isn't a bug in the SQL Server driver?

If I understand PR #434 correctly, it would set the column size to 2 for the example one-character string.  I get it is because each UCS2 "character" is 2-bytes, but that is not true of UTF-16 which is a variable length encoding.

This really sounds like SQL Server is being a bit lazy and treating UTF-16 like UCS2.  When I ran an insert in SQL Server Management Studio, `select len(v)` returned 2, which really isn't correct.  Though, I was unable to get it to display as a half-moon, so I'm not entirely sure that was a good test.). Is the issue perhaps SQL Server does not support Unicode characters that are larger than 2-bytes?

Right now, databases set to use UTF-8 are not failing, but I think they could with this change.  (Though most seem much more tolerant of input, so we might get lucky.). If it really is just SQL Server when using wchar_t, perhaps we just need a special check for utf-16.

Also, I'd recommend we look at using the encoding constants.  There are some constants for the different UTF-16 which is safer than looking at the string.  After all, the encoding is [officially "utf_16"](https://docs.python.org/2/library/codecs.html) in Python with aliases like "U16" and "utf16" that might get missed by looking for strings.

(Beware of using ctype.  It can be set to 2 when writing UTF-8 to a wchar_t buffer.  That's just what some of the databases require.)

The timeout isn't ignored -- it is used to set [SQL_ATTR_LOGIN_TIMEOUT](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/ms715455(v=vs.85).aspx).  There is also a SQL_ATTR_CONNECTION_TIMEOUT that could be set, so perhaps I should set it too.

The SQL_ATTR_CONNECTION_TIMEOUT affects all queries, not just connection attempts, so I was trying to keep them separate.  Right now it is only set *after* connecting when you use ``cnxn.timeout=4``.

I tested setting both before a login with SQL Server on Windows and PostgreSQL on OS X and neither worked.  This seems to be a bug in the drivers.

I'm going to close this, but if you can find any information please reopen.  I'm happy to put a fix in if there is one.

For the travis file you just add:
```yaml
cache:
  directories:
    - $HOME/conda

@ribeaud So, here's how I'd propose to convert this to a helpful feature in the next release or two, tell me what you think:

**Implementation:**

Add a 'form' element to the ContentHandler class, so that you can supply, read from file, and template out form data as a request body.  This is internally URL encoded and concatenated with separators per the HTTP spec, as well as setting the default header (unless you choose to override this).


**Test syntax:**

```yaml
- test:
  - name: "I submit form data'
  - method: POST
  - body: {form: {'parameter': 'value', 'cheese': 'gouda', 'foo': 'bar'}}
```

And it would do all the above implicitly under the hood in the configure_curl method (or a helper method more likely, to keep that short).

With this you'd get template/variable binding integration, the ability to read from file, current encoding handling goodies, etc.  The catch is that it adds one more dimension to the combinatorial matrix that is the ContentHandler.

**Timeline:**

Currently I'm in the midst of major revisions to support Python 3, handle encoding more safely, and offer better support for dynamic content passing.  These would all drive potentially significant changes to the same code, so this is going to come after that (unless someone in the community has time to put together a PR).  

Since there's a way to do it currently (albeit clumsily) and side project time is limited at the moment, probably we're looking at the release *after* next unless I get a burst of time or a PR comes in.

I apologize for the delayed response here (due to my job taking most of my coding time).  Unfortunately, this isn't supported at the moment, though there are plans to add it.  There are two routes:

1. Add templating for these variables to the core tests.py class, so they may be derived from context variables.  The templating mechanism here is a bit hacky, but it is easy enough to add this. 
2. Scoped configuration (which allows inheritance of configuration from higher-level options): https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/issues/45 

I would prefer to implement this properly via option 2, and at the same time allow any field to be templated, since it provides a global fix for this and any other fields someone may want to template enable.. but it requires considerably more coding to implement.

Thank you, I'm glad you've found it helpful!  Yes, you can do OAuth with PyrestTest, it provides all the necessary components. 

The specific code will depend on how your API token is obtained and used, since each provider is a bit different.  But in general, you'll do the following:

1. Call the authorization API, passing authentication information (basic auth, request body, or headers). 2. For the authorization call above, define an extractor to retrieve the API token and information and store it to a variable for later use in requests.
2. Use the authorization token in calls, with templated headers to insert it using the variable (or templated URL using it as a query parameter, or templating of a request body if it is supplied there).

Something like this is easy to convert from curl calls to PyCurl:
https://gist.github.com/caspyin/2288960

The only catch to remember is that some APIs will need URL encoded inputs, which PyRestTest doesn't have an automatic filter for (no converter functions to do automatic encoding/decoding, since there hasn't been a call for them yet, so that has to be done manually or by custom extensions). 

Does that help answer your question?  If you've got a particular documented API, I can provide some tips about how to do this most effectively with PyRestTest

That's the correct answer. Lackey will treat the string as a single command, so if there are spaces, the path has to be enclosed in quotes *within* the string to make sure the operating system recognizes it as a single token:

    [C:\Program] [Files\iTunes\iTunes.exe]

vs

    ["C:\Program Files\iTunes\iTunes.exe"]

This looks like it's operating as designed. Unless you have any concerns, I think we can close out this issue?
I solved by using app = lackey.App(r'"C:\\Program Files\\iTunes\\iTunes.exe"').open()

Note that doing this *creates a shared reference to the instance of `Ask` between all instances of `MyClass`!*

An example:

```python
#!/bin/bash python

class person:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

class junk:
    p = person('dave')


a = junk()
b = junk()

print('a.p.name: %s, b.p.name: %s' % (a.p.name, b.p.name))
print(a.p)
print(b.p)
print(a.p is b.p)

print("changing name of person on 'a'")
a.p.name = 'dan'
print('a.p.name: %s, b.p.name: %s' % (a.p.name, b.p.name))
```

Prints:
```
$python example.py
a.p.name: dave, b.p.name: dave
<__main__.person object at 0x10369cf60>
<__main__.person object at 0x10369cf60>
True
changing name of person on 'a'
a.p.name: dan, b.p.name: dan
```

This behavior is slightly different with built-in literals due to how the interpreter handles them vs. objects. (That's a longer explanation.)

Hi all,

You can hack through this issue by replacing the tag in the response string before passing to xmltodict parser.
For example, if your text is "<someTag>bar foo</someTag>" and you do not want it to be parsed, you can try xmltodict.parse(resp.text.replace("<someTag>", "").replace("</someTag>", "")).
This simple hack does not solve all problems, but it solves mine.

I have been thinking to add support for widely-accepted CAD formats for importing and exporting B-Spline and NURBS curves & surfaces. CAD interoperability is a problem and I feel like adding JSON support to NURBS-Python would not do any help for 2 reasons:

* It looks like adding JSON support is implementing another CAD format, which doesn't technically help on the CAD interoperability issue
* People will still prefer to use existing CAD formats instead of the JSON format discussed in this issue

It is very hard to add support for proprietary formats but I think it is possible to add support for IGES and STEP (and maybe 3DM) which are very common among CAD users. However, it is going to take time for me to implement import and export functionality for these formats but eventually, we will get these support in NURBS-Python.

On the other hand, I would like to introduce a load-save functionality in BSpline and NURBS modules before the common CAD format support is implemented. It currently uses python's native `pickle` module. I know `pickle` might not be the best choice but it is a very simple choice and it should ease the data transfer problems a little, at least between NURBS-Python users.

The load-save functionality will be released with NURBS-Python v4.0

`from pyModbusTCP.client import ModbusClient
from pyModbusTCP import utils

c = ModbusClient(host="localhost", port=502, auto_open=True)

The largest input files I've ever run through this myself are in the order of ~10Mb. It has always performed well enough for my purposes, but I'm open to feedback on how performance might be improved.

That said, if your objective is just to get data from a ArcGIS rest service into PostGIS, this may not be the best tool to help you. Converting to GeoJSON as an intermediate process is going to make your process slower rather than faster whichever way you slice it. Simplifying the geographies will be best done either in ArcGIS or PostGIS rather than when serialized as JSON.

However, I will see if I can provide a couple of tips:

1. I guess the first thing I'd suggest would be to see if you can get the ArcGIS server to do the work for you if possible. If you can have the service produce simplified outputs rather than producing complex outputs and having to simplify them yourself, that is going to help. Look into https://developers.arcgis.com/rest/services-reference/simplify.htm or https://developers.arcgis.com/rest/services-reference/generalize.htm

2. The other question I'd ask is can you break the problem down at all? If you've got one very large detailed polygon you won't be able to, but if you've got a large FeatureCollection composed of lots of polygons, you could request it in chunks from the web service. (e.g: by calling `/query?where=objectid%3D1`, `/query?where=objectid%3D2`.. or something) to give you smaller files that are easier to deal with one-by-one.

Best of luck with your project. If you can provide more specific feedback on performance problems with this package or more details/example of an input that results in performance issues, feel free to add it to this thread.

Contexts doesn't run `when` or `then` methods from the base class - only `given` and `cleanup` methods. This is by design. I have seen plenty of tests which are hard to understand because the acting or asserting logic is hidden in a base class, so Contexts strongly encourages you to put your `when` and `then` methods in the test class itself. The idea is that the methods of a test case read like a sentence, which isn't possible when half the sentence is missing!

A workaround that seems to work for me (without detailed testing) is to redeclare `objects` on the subclass. This then results in the subclass's default manager being MultilingualManager.

E.g., using my examples from the first comment of this issue:

```
class BazAbstract(models.Model):
    objects = models.Manager()
    class Meta:
        abstract = True

class Baz(BazAbstract):
    objects = models.Manager()  # <- This has been added
    name = models.CharField(blank=True, max_length=1)

# Django 1.10 output:
Baz <class 'django.db.models.manager.MultilingualManager'>
```

(I'm redeclaring `objects` on `Baz` rather than just moving it off `BazAbstract` to `Baz` because in my real models the abstract class is being used by other non-modeltranslation enabled classes and I don't want to touch them (and, in general, one might not be able to change the abstract class anyway).)

Fingers crossed for a fix, Model Translation is so useful!

From an implementation point of view, it's hard to enable such mono-dimensional array. 

The 1-D shape could be passed directly to the backend, whereas all the sparse operators in the backend  assumes `shape[0]` is num_rows and `shape[1]` is num_cols at the moment. Accepting 1-D shape for csr would require **significant efforts** to check these cases for each operator in the backend. `zeros` is not the only operator affected, cases include:
- `mx.sym.var('a', shape=10, stype='csr')`
- generate a random uniform csr ndarray with 1-D shape 
- dot with 1-D csr
- convert a dense 1-D array to csr
- etc..

I suggest that we throw an exception for 1-D shape for `sparse.zeros` to guide users the expected usage instead.

Most of Mxnet computation are asynchronous. 
A simple loop like this will crash GPU:
```python
for image in dataset:
     tmps += module.predict(image)
```
Because it start new prediction without waiting the end of previous one.

To force mxnet to wait the end of previous prediction you can add `waitall`
```python
for image in dataset:
     tmps += module.predict(image)
     ndarray.waitall()
```

here is my function for remapping special characters : 

**ascii_converter.py**

```
import numpy as np

########################################################################################
#
#   A simple routine for some non-ascii conversion.
#   I'm limited to [a-z] letters
#   letter 'é' is seen as 99, 73   so need ti remap 73 and erase 99
#   for use in DEEPSPEECH project
#
########################################################################################


def read(data):

    result = data
    My_counter = 0
    #print('start_datas '+str(result))
    for i in result:
        if i == 99: # é = 99 73
            result=np.delete(result, My_counter)
            My_counter-=1
        if i == 73: #('é')
            result[My_counter]=27
        if i == 72: #('è')
            result[My_counter]=28
        if i == 74: #('ê')
            result[My_counter]=29
        if i == 64: #('à')	
            result[My_counter]=30
        if i == 66: #('â')
            result[My_counter]=31
        if i == 71: #('ç')
            result[My_counter]=32
        if i == 91: #('û')
            result[My_counter]=33
        if i == 89: #('ù')
            result[My_counter]=34
        if i == 78: #('î')
            result[My_counter]=35
        if i == 84: #('ô')
            result[My_counter]=36
        if i == -51: #('-')
            result[My_counter]=37
        My_counter+=1

    #print('remapped_datas '+str(result))

    return(result)
```

It will return "True" if the request succeeds, and an error otherwise. (Type: Boolean)

Do you want the requirements that needs to be installed for this environment? Or all requirements (even if their requirement markers don't match) ? What about extras?

For the former, you can use `pkg_resources` after running `./setup.py egg_info`:

```python
import pkg_resources

dist = next(pkg_resources.find_distributions('.'))
print('\n'.join(sorted(map(str, dist.requires()))))
```

To get all requirements you could use a custom distutil command:
```python
from distutils.core import Command

import pkg_resources


class show_requires(Command):

    description = "show project requirements"
    user_options = []

    def initialize_options(self):
        pass

    def finalize_options(self):
        pass

    def run(self):
        requires = []
        install_requires = getattr(self.distribution, 'install_requires', [])
        requires.extend(pkg_resources.parse_requirements(install_requires))
        extras_require = getattr(self.distribution, 'extras_require', {})
        for extra, reqs in extras_require.items():
            if not extra.startswith(':'):
                continue
            marker = extra[1:]
            e = pkg_resources.invalid_marker(marker)
            if e:
                raise e
            for r in pkg_resources.parse_requirements(reqs):
                assert not r.marker
                r.marker = marker
                requires.append(r)
        print('\n'.join(sorted(map(str, requires))))
```
Other invalid characters will trigger an error.

I mean that in order to generate a dump that look like it was written by a human, you will need AST access, which JS-YAML cannot provide. It is possible to add option that will accept object of the same structure as data to dump and write it's values as comments, but I don't think that it will have much sense, and I don't think I like the idea of adding such weird stuff to the library.

As a very primitive manner, I have made the following code for check:

```python
def my_make_source_mask(data, snr, npixels, mask=None, mask_value=None,
                     filter_fwhm=None, filter_size=3, filter_kernel=None,
                     sigclip_sigma=3.0, sigclip_iters=5, dilate_size=11,
                     gain=1.0, readnoise=0.0, use_snr_map=False):

    from scipy import ndimage
    from photutils import detect_threshold, detect_sources
    from astropy.stats import gaussian_fwhm_to_sigma
    from astropy.convolution import Gaussian2DKernel
    threshold = detect_threshold(data, snr, background=None, error=None,
                                 mask=mask, mask_value=None,
                                 sigclip_sigma=sigclip_sigma,
                                 sigclip_iters=sigclip_iters)

    kernel = None
    if filter_kernel is not None:
        kernel = filter_kernel
    if filter_fwhm is not None:
        sigma = filter_fwhm * gaussian_fwhm_to_sigma
        kernel = Gaussian2DKernel(sigma, x_size=filter_size,
                                  y_size=filter_size)
    if kernel is not None:
        kernel.normalize()

    if use_snr_map:
        snr_map = make_snr_map(data, msky=None,
                               gain=gain, readnoise=readnoise,
                               sigclip_sigma=sigclip_sigma,
                               sigclip_iters= sigclip_iters)
        segm = detect_sources(snr_map, threshold=snr, npixels=npixels, filter_kernel=kernel)
    else:
        segm = detect_sources(data, threshold, npixels, filter_kernel=kernel)

    selem = np.ones((dilate_size, dilate_size))
    
    return ndimage.binary_dilation(segm.data.astype(np.bool), selem)


def make_snr_map(data, gain=1.0, readnoise=0.0, msky=None, 
                 sigclip_sigma=3, sigclip_iters=10):
    datacp = data.copy()

    if msky==None:
        avg, med, std = sigma_clipped_stats(data, sigma=sigclip_sigma, iters=sigclip_iters)
        msky = med

    signal = datacp - msky
    noise = np.sqrt(datacp / gain + (readnoise / gain)**2)
    return signal/noise
```

However, for further discussions (probably I should open issues for this ;-):

* Fully agree that control number should be a string, since MARC21 definition allows more than numbers.
* I think the minter (``recid_minter``) should likely be moved to MARC21 package, and probably renamed to something like ``controlnumber_minter``.
* ``controlnumber_minter``: We ought to get away from incrementing integers as primary identifiers in Invenio. IMHO we should only keep them for backward compatibility, since they were so central in previous Invenio installations.

ARG ES_VERSION=2

Actually no.  Although multi stars have theie motions correlated, 
particularly at low frequency, ALL stars move.  An individual star's 
angular motion relative to a fixed point on the sky (where the telescope 
is tracking) has components to its apparent position coming from each 
screen. The lowest screen (ground layer) causes a low frequency ~1 Hz 
wander (called "wander seeing"). Of course this is easiest for PCA to 
take out since it is highly correlated across a chip.  Turbulent cells 
in higher layers generate speckles, and that component of the star 
centroid motion is at higher frequencies.

A few minutes of staring at the guide star is enough to decide how much 
power in the seeing motion is coming from low vs upper layers.  The 
ratio tends not to change much during a night unless a front comes through.

At very low frequency (~0.1 Hz) the culprit is often bad mirror 
temperature (higher than ambient).  This is separate from the ground layer.

I just discovered [-stdlib=libstdc++](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/19774778/when-is-it-necessary-to-use-use-the-flag-stdlib-libstdc) which successfully builds tmv against libstdc++:
```
scons CXX=g++ EXTRA_FLAGS='-stdlib=libstdc++' LINKFLAGS='-stdlib=libstdc++' install
```
I'm not sure which of these options is actually required, but all together they do the job.

The corresponding updated GalSim install cmd is:
```
scons TMV_DIR=/usr/local FFTW_DIR=/usr/local PREFIX=$HOME/anaconda3/envs/galsim CXX=g++ EXTRA_FLAGS='-stdlib=libstdc++' LINKFLAGS='-stdlib=libstdc++' install
```
This works and I can import the resulting galsim library (when using the galsim conda environment)!

Note that clang now complains with:
```
clang: warning: libstdc++ is deprecated; move to libc++ [-Wdeprecated]
```
but that's the price you pay for not installing boost from source (which seems to be the main stumbling block for most new users).

> how do I include the Poisson code in GalSim and yet keep it up to date if I push new code in my own repository?

The standard answer would be to make your code available as a python library that GalSim can just import as needed if the user tries to call that function.  Then when you have an update to your code, you would release a new version, and users could grab that separately from GalSim, which would then be using your new version.

I could help set up the wrapping for you so the function we need would be callable from python.  It would also not be too hard to make it pip installable, which would be convenient.  (More convenient than the Boost Python wrapping scheme we currently use in GalSim.)

Another option would be to periodically sync up the version in GalSim with what you have in your repo.  You'd only really need to do that when we put out a new release of GalSim, and our release schedule isn't terribly frequent, so it probably wouldn't be too onerous.  e.g. When you make a significant update to your code, you could post an issue titled something like "Update the Poisson solver to the current version before releasing GalSim version 1.x".  Then when we get around to making a release, we'll ping you to merge in whatever the latest stable version is at that point (which may include even more updates since you posted the issue).

I think either mode would be fine, so whichever you prefer.

Hi - @rmjarvis is right that the WCS is going to updated due to some Cycle 5 -> 6 updates.  You can find a preview on the branch that he mentioned.  There are a few updates:

- it seems there might be a slight overall offset of the entire focal plane compared to what there should be
- in cycle 5->6, they swapped around 2 rows of SCA labels

I am waiting to hear from some collaborators to confirm that the new code is OK, but after that, it will be updated.

However, it seems you had two somewhat separate issues.  It seems like the parity flip issue might not have been real (from your update in the initial issue) but it sounds like your first concern was a difficulty in making a correspondence between some position that you want to observe, and the actual SCA it lives on?  Have I understood this correctly?

As outlined in the docstring for ```galsim.wfirst.getWCS()```, the ```world_pos``` argument does indeed correspond to the center of the focal plane as on the diagram here:
https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/wiki/GalSim-WFIRST-module-diagrams
(marked as a plus sign inside a circle)
Currently, there is no option to specify that ```world_pos``` corresponds to something else, like the center of one of the SCAs.  In your shoes, I would probably do the following:

1. Choose some central position in your catalog as the center of the focal plane array, and get the full set of WCSs for all the SCAs.
2. For each object in the catalog, use its position along with the ```galsim.wfirst.findSCA``` routine to figure out which SCA it lives on (if any; it might of course be in a gap or off the edge).
3. Then use the WCS for the SCA that was identified in step (2) to get GalSim to draw the object in the right image, with the right location and orientation.

If your catalog covers a decent area, this should be fine.  If it's a tiny area, like less than a single SCA, this is probably a really clunky way to do it as you have too high a chance to end up with most of the objects in a gap.

Does that make sense?  Sorry, I may not have fully grasped what you are trying to do, but I'm happy to discuss further if needed.

This also needed with google.golang.org imports. This URL returns the following two meta tags upon import:
```
<meta name="go-import" content="google.golang.org/api git https://code.googlesource.com/google-api-go-client">

<meta name="go-source" content="google.golang.org/api https://github.com/google/google-api-go-client https://github.com/google/google-api-go-client/tree/master{/dir} https://github.com/google/google-api-go-client/tree/master{/dir}/{file}#L{line}">
```

The biggest issue here is that the repo is supposed to be rooted a directory below your import. So, if you pull in something like `google.golang.org/api/googleapi` as part of your imports you download all of the github.com/google/google-api-go-client which contains a `googleapi` directory. So this repo should be unarchived in `google.golang.org/api` instead of `google.golang.org/api/googleapi` which the meta tag indicates for "go-import".

Just adding a banned character will be easier to insert into the codebase. The current practice is hardcoded in a across the codebase, all of which would need to hook into the Address class in order for fingerprinting to work.

This will involve injecting the ban into the build file parser, which is probably a little bit of a leak, or maybe override the ban within SyntheticAddress. The current practice is to use the transformed spec as a target name for codegen which means that the transformed character has to be allowed in SyntheticAddresses. I could change that practice but then we are back to making a fingerprint for the spec again because of potential collisions with whatever we used as a codegen name.

I like banning the colon since that is at least generally shunned (if not banned) in unix filenames anyway.

I've renamed the feature to add_bookmark only. As it looks like it's a better idea to first fully focus on the correct implementation of the bookmark object, and afterwards see how things like captions and cross references interact with the bookmarks.  

## More detailed VBA function analysis
Like you recommended, I've investigated the VBA bookmark [documentation](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/vba/word-vba/articles/bookmark-object-word). This provided some more background and insight about what kind of behavior and functionality is expected from the bookmark object.

### Bookmarks
A collection of bookmark object that represent the bookmarks in the specified selection, range, or document. Also, the object returns the bookmark corresponding to the index, which is either a key or a number. The numbered index can be used, and the named indices can apparently be sorted alphabetically.  
There is also a set of predefined bookmarks - not sure whether these can be accessed by the package. 

### Bookmark methods:
There are three methods described:
1. Copy - copy method of the bookmark, requires a new name as input, which will subsequently be the name of the copied bookmark.
2. Delete - Deletes the bookmark from the bookmarks object.
3. Select - Selects the part spanned by the bookmark. (is this even possible to implement in this package?) The select method returns a 'Selection' object, which in turn can be used to apply certain formatter.

### Bookmark properties:
There is a total of ten properties:
1. Application - Returns the word application 
2. Column - Boolean, True if specified bookmark is a table column. 
3. Creator - Integer indicating the application which was used to create the object.
4. Empty - True if specified bookmark is empty
5. End - Returns or sets the ending character position of a selection, range, or bookmark. Read/write  [link](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/vba/word-vba/articles/bookmark-end-property-word)
6. Name - Returns the name of the bookmark
7. Parent - Returns the parent object of the specified bookmark object.
8. Range - Returns a Range object that represents the portion of a document that's contained in the specified object.
9. Start - Returns or sets the starting character position of a bookmark. Read/write
10. StoryType - Returns the story type for the specified range, selection, or bookmark. Read-only

### Properties Included or Omitted
I think features 1, 3, 10 can be omitted, and I'm unsure about 8. 
This leaves features, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9.  Which is already a nice list.

I've been thinking about this a little.  Consider a setting which looks like this:

```
IMAGE_SIZES = [
    ('100x50', 'Mobile'),
    ('200x100', 'iPad'),
    ('400x200', 'Desktop'),
]
```

Then a new model in `cms.apps.media.models` which looks like this:

```
class FileVariant(models.Model):

    file = models.ForeignKey(
        File,
        on_delete=models.DELETE,
        related_name='+',
    )

    size = models.CharField(
        max_length=100,
        choices=settings.IMAGE_SIZES,
    )
```

And a method on the existing `File` model:

```
def get_variant(self, size):
    variant = self.filevariant_set.filter(
        size=size,
    )

    if variant.exists():
        return variant

    return self
```

You could then use `ImageRefField` as normal throughout the project, then in your frontend code, instead of doing `obj.image.file.file`, you would do `obj.image.get_variant('100x50')` and it would return the file you need - whether that be the variant at your exact resolution, or the source file from the original `File` object.

> readline() goes through C stdio which makes it impossible to get

Hi @fakufaku thanks for this answer! So if I understand correctly, the "correct way" to do FD filtering would be this:

 - do a regular STFT of size N without zero-padding to get analysis data
 - compute the beamforming weights (for instance for MVDR, GEV) from this, which will give weights which are of length N, and corresponding to a time-domain convolution of length N
 - then do a second STFT of size 2N with N elements zero-padded (or 2N elements and use overlap-save) and filter with the N weights zero-padded to 2N

this seems a bit computation-hungry though, and I never saw this done in any codebase I saw, so this is why I was wondering if this was the "correct way" to do it, or if there are reasons why someone can do the beamforming without zero-padding?

I was assuming that the justification would be that if we do hops of size L << N and we recover only the L central coefficients, and that the data has been windowed, then the influence of the circular convolution on those L elements would be small, and we wouldn't need to do the 2N zero-padding?

If someone's working on the compatibility with nx 1.9.1: one bug seems to be when nx (in its code) tries to save the graph that was passed as argument. The statement is `subG = type(G) (G.edges_iter())`. Probably this leads to G being constructed again (by calling add_edge()) and causes an infinite loop. 
One hack to solve this is to pass the graph to (for example) nx.simple_cycles() as `nx.simple_cycles(nx.DiGraph(self))` rather than `nx.simple_cycles(self)`.

The DAQmx API allows configuring the task to dump right to a file without having to need.  For example you can do the following:

`import nidaqmx
import nidaqmx.constants
import time


if __name__ == "__main__":
    with nidaqmx.Task() as task:
        task.ai_channels.add_ai_voltage_chan(
            "PXI1Slot2/ai0")
        task.in_stream.configure_logging(
            r"c:\Users\nstoddar\Desktop\task.tdms",
            nidaqmx.constants.LoggingMode.LOG)
        task.timing.cfg_samp_clk_timing(
            1000,
            samps_per_chan=1000)
        task.start()
        while not task.is_task_done():
            time.sleep(0.5)
`
This example is just a single finite acquisition for 1 second but will log all the data to disk.  You can alternatively choose LOG_AND_READ to cause the driver to log to the file every time you call read.  This is lower performance but allows you to both log the data and access it directly in the program.

I think you're misunderstanding what ELB is.

ELB - Elastic Load Balancer, sits in front of your instances, and proxys incoming data to instances:

![ELB architecture](https://media.amazonwebservices.com/blog/2014/elb_instances_1.png)

You will configure your DNS to point at the load balancer, and the load balancer will distribute incoming requests to your instances by round robin (e.g. first request goes to instance A, second request to instance B, third request to instance C, forth request to instance A, and so on so forth). 

You see multiple IP addresses because there are multiple ELB instances running, both to distribute the load among multiple ELBs, and to handle failure.

If you need to SSH into the instances, (maintenance, introspection, or similar) you do so directly, and not via the load balancer.

I've faced the same issue. It was caused because a file was opened by other process and since my Windows is pt-br (brazilian Portuguese) the exception message was:

```python
WindowsError(32, 'O arquivo j\xe1 est\xe1 sendo usado por outro processo')
```

That message as consequence caused the line in `move_path_to_trash` method to fail because of coercion to ASCII:

```python
log.debug("Could not move %s to %s (%s)" % (path, trash_dir, e))
```

Error handling involving unicode characters is indeed a troublesome and error prone issue.

OK, I've got it. It's the damn timestamps. (Sort of) The relevant optimization passes here are, in order: 1. Maximize the version numbers of the requested packages (in this case, m2-file, conda, conda-env) 2. Maximize the build number (and now, _timestamp_) of the requested packages 3. Minimize the number of installed packages that need updating. Before the advent of timestamps, `conda-4.3.29-py35hc07314a_0` and `conda-4.3.29-py36h7c1b203_0` would have equal consideration after Step 1 and 2, but the `py35` package would be eliminated from consideration by Step 3. Now that timestamps are considered, `conda-4.3.29-py35hc07314a_0` is actually the _only_ survivor of Step 2. So now, Step 3 _has_ to schedule downgrades to Python 3.5 in order to install it. Clearly, this is not the behavior we want. I think there are two choices here. _Fix A:_ Swap the order of steps 2 and 3.
_Fix B:_ _Break apart_ Step 2 into Step 2a (build number) and Step 2b (timestamp), and put Step 2b after Step 3. The simplest fix is clearly Fix A. But I think that's not the correct one overall. If someone issues a new build of a package `foo` with a dependency change, then someone doing `conda update foo` is not going to get that latest build, because conda will prefer to keep all of their dependencies the same. So I think we _need_ to do Fix B.

@monkeybutter, most of the comments by @FrancescElies are correct, but I think you are not comparing pears with pears here.  The thing that takes 20 ms in h5py is reading a contiguous, non-compressed HDF5 array.  This is fast.  But the chunked and compressed version of the array is in cells 5 and 6 (with a different chunksize): this is what is comparable with bcolz (the storage is chunked and compressed by default).  In the case, the speed of bcolz is almost ~2x faster than h5py (~80 ms vs ~40 ms), and this is the expected figure.  Of course, I don't think this is due to differences in peformance of HDF5 vs bcolz, but rather just due to Blosc performance.  For example, PyTables can use HDF5 in combination with Blosc, and I would predict that performance would be similar than bcolz in this case.

As @FrancescElies pointed out, you can play with tuning several knobs in bcolz for getting more performance (using different compression levels, different internal compressors, different number of threads, different chunk lengths, and so on and so forth).  But it is important to remember that you are probably using a misleading comparison because probably your data is already in the cache of your OS (which is my guess for the scenario in your benchmark).  However, and because both lzf and Blosc are very fast in comparison with hard disks, when you are reading *from disk* you will end having speed-ups that are very close to the compression ratio (in our example above, if the compression ratio is ~2x, then your expected speed-up would be ~2x roughly too).  With SSDs things may be different and Blosc might start to show some noticeable advantage wrt other compressors (e.g. lzf).  For in-memory decompressions (i.e. your likely scenario above) Blosc should have a *clear* advantage in general.

Finally, bcolz is meant to store data compressed either in-memory or on-disk, and that means that you can squeeze the most of it when you operate its containers (carray, ctable) either using [iterators](https://bcolz.readthedocs.org/en/latest/reference.html#iter) or [block-iterators](https://bcolz.readthedocs.org/en/latest/reference.html#iterblocks), or using blocked algorithms via [eval](https://bcolz.readthedocs.org/en/latest/reference.html#eval) or just via external packages like [bquery](https://github.com/visualfabriq/bquery) or [dask](https://github.com/blaze/dask).

Ämne: [OpenCobolIDE] Cobol Project Support (#84)

 

I am opening this issue to discuss the possibility to add project support to
the IDE.

OpenCobolIDE has been designed to work on a single file basic. You open a
file and compile it. Well, this is not entirely true, actually there is a
sort of implicit project support: the IDE will first parse the file you want
to compile to detect its dependencies and compile them before compiling the
file itself.

Not having a project concept makes the IDE very easy to use for a beginner
(there is no makefile, not config to setup) but can be very limitating for
the experts.

Introducing the concept of project would bring the following benefits:

*	ability to specify which file should be compiled and how. With the
current design, the compiler properties are applied globally on every file
that get compiled.
*	ability to add VCS support (#83
<https://github.com/OpenCobolIDE/OpenCobolIDE/issues/83> )

A very important point to consider when implementing this feature is that it
is very important to keep the ability to open and compile a single file.
This means that we must have two different kind of windows: a single
document window that you will use to work on a single unit (mainly for the
beginners) and a project view with vcs integration and all the bells and
whistles a project can offer. This means a complete redesign of the
application!

Also if we implement project support, we need to find a build system for
building the project sources but I am not sure if there is a standard build
system for cobol (I don't want a makefile because IMHO this goes against the
philosophy of an IDE where you should be able to add new source files
without editing anything, using pure GUI actions).

Finally got an example working in Java/Groovy thanks to some help on StackOverflow. 

[http://stackoverflow.com/questions/39112101/java-8-time-equivalent-of-net-datetime-maxvalue-ticks/39112535?noredirect=1#comment65572581_39112535](url)

In short, from a /metricDefinition result, you can take the : partition key , table name, sasToken, and tableEndpoint.  With those, you can construct most of the HTTP parameters you need to query the storage API for the actual metric values. 

The hard part (outside of .NET), is replicating the math needed to generate the "RowKey" parameters.  You can see the solution Andreas came up with in Java. 

The RowKey column essentially acts as an indexed timestamp column.   You really need to include RowKey parameters in your call, because it's the only indexed column (other than partitionKey). If you don't include it, each query does a full partition scan, and queries can take several minutes. 

Note: The azure storage table has an actual timestamp column, but it's not indexed. You need to include RowKey. 

[http://stackoverflow.com/questions/19715885/azure-tables-query-by-rowkey-as-condition](url)

Good luck to you!

We could consider doing something like this:

`font-family: system-ui, sans-serif;`

`system-ui` is now [in the CSS draft](https://drafts.csswg.org/css-fonts-4/#system-ui-def), so browsers which support it would use that, and the user-customizable `sans-serif` could then serve as an immediate fallback, as opposed to trying to guess explicitly which system fonts to use on various systems.

The reason I wouldn’t reach straight for the `sans-serif` is that for most users this may be difficult to change to use the system UI font. On a Mac for example, San Francisco is hidden from users.

Another pro of moving in this direction is that most system UI fonts support more grades of `font-weight` than the default sans, which gives a little more flexibility to optimize legibility in the UI.

This tells Hyperledger that you are acknowledging that you can't contribute IP encumbered by a patent, that you own copyright to what you commit, etc. See https://github.com/probot/dco#how-it-works. > I have some concerns about the performance of this implementation:

This is designed as a simple use case, intended to be understandable at a glance. I don't expect it will be the most performant or even work well for downloading a 10,000 file project.

> Each time you reference a project by name we have to fetch the list of all projects and iterate through them. In practice it takes about 3 seconds to fetch the list of projects. If David is going to use this to download/upload 10K files this would add about 8 hours to the total time.

For that use case, we could encourage listing projects and files with their IDs, then fetching by ID. I suggested that for name conflicts but it would help avoid additional lookups too. Or we could have an object that worked more like a `requests.session`, but cached these listings for performance gains. Either way, I think it's fair if the performance gains have a cost of increased complexity. I don't think the increased complexity should be the default operating mode, and would expect advanced users to make that tradeoff.

While the analysis core is one audience that we hope will use this library, I think we should be focusing on the simplest possible interface that works, only adding complexity as needed. More demanding users can use the well-factored but appropriately complex internal python code or make HTTP calls directly.

So here's what I'm looking for. Potentially Peru isn't the right tool for this but I think it might be. The other tool I'm considering is Ansible but the concept of being able to keep development dependencies in sync with a single command is very appealing.

I have several teams (currently 7) that are all independently working on several different projects. These teams all have a lot of cross over in what they're doing but not enough to consider consolidating them. Often one team will use a component in a different part of their technology stack or they'll use it to push / pull data for very different reasons. Two examples is we have teams that read server logs looking for failures on customer clusters and a team that's looking for failures on development builds of clusters. All of these use cases are very similar but we found having a single repository to be incredibly bloated.

But they all share puzzle pieces. It used to be the case that every team would just build everything. But this lead to a lot of redundant work (calculated in developer-years).

So we have two parser project, two translation layer projects (works not done consolidating), several different provisioning projects, shell clients, analytics layers, database inserters, alerts systems, etc. 

Some projects share share just one component. Some projects use almost all of them. Almost all of these projects are under active development. That's really the key behind my ask, the fact that everything is worked on. The above example lead to tens of thousands of redundant lines of code. It was a hot mess. 

git submodules and subtree's don't work very well with our use case.. 

Submodules turn super project logs into

    d61f574 Update submodule to increment git describe
    6b2ea1f updating submodule
    eb04ed4 Updating submodules after checking out the right branch in each folder
    59b93da Updating submodule pointers
    d648aaf Updating pointers for submodules.
    16db200 Updating submodule references in master project.
    37a1ea0 Add missing submodules
    3d4f244 Update packaging submodule
    83c2d9c Update packaging submodules.
    612da3b Remove old submodules
    da9ed6a Add Debian/Ubuntu packaging submodules.
    bc07065 Use absolute URL for submodules
    42c0c17 Add submodule for RPM packages

Submodules are fundamentally broken when you have to change the commit your targeted against weekly if not daily. This leads to a nightmare when you're trying to track down what changes happened and when.

Subtree's have a different problem. Merge conflicts kill us and often we want to work in a development project, in a subproject, to see how that change affects the super project. More often than not our development will be happening in branches. It's also a common case that we have to pull another teams super project if our change affects them in a breaking way.

Potentially git just doesn't support this workflow and I need to look into different revision control systems.

Hello @oconnor663, thanks for taking the time to explain things for me. There's a couple of other problems with large projects that I don't think there are good solutions to currently.

* It's very hard to build structural trust into a large repository

    Let's take an extreme example. There are many operating systems that use Python for user space programs. If, it was the case, that Python had grown out of the Arch Linux community and was a directory in the Arch source code then it would be 1) probably more work to pull the Python code base out of Arch for use in other projects and 2) the Arch core contributors and the Python core contributors would have to have full write access to both projects.

    Now this might not be a bad thing if both projects have. Potentially all individuals involved are awesome individuals and no harm will come out of this arrangement. But the only trust system in this arrangement is your (or other owners) judgement of individuals. Your human trust in them.

    Human trust severally limits who you can add to a project. Most projects keep write privileges close to their chest because of the damage write privileges can cause.

    An example of this is `youtube.com`. There's no way to add individual users or teams to a channel. There's just one user and one password per channel. So if multiple people work on a channel everyone gets everything all the time.

* The lack of logical partitioning makes it much harder to on board new developers 

    We're not at the size of Facebook or Google but we're also not a small. As of the time of this writing I'm an owner of a project that contains ~95,000 lines of Python and C code that's had ~500,000 additions and ~ 400,000 deletions in it's lifetime. We act as the lowest unit for several projects built on top of us. I don't know how many LOC it would produce to combine all projects but it would be very large.

    Combining all of our projects would be a major barrier to entry. It's already a lot of code to wrap your head around and the control flow isn't always the best as is (all projects acquire cruft). 

* Large repositories disincentive creating module code

    [My team has been moving more and more towards a micro service approach to software.](http://martinfowler.com/articles/microservices.html) This can create some performance headaches down the road since it heavily incentives message passing but the benefits (as far as we see them) for creating components with well defined interfaces that are easy to plug and play are large enough that they out weight the cons. Creating a large repository means that, rather than just git cloning a different component, you actually have to make configuration or code changes to use a different component.

    It's also just plain easier to deal with well defined interfaces than it is to modify configurations.

* One large repository becomes ungainly when you have different deployment scenarios

    This one is the real killer for us. We sell storage clusters to customers and they manage the entire system without us being able to gain access to their network. That's one our big selling points, they own everything.

   The super project that uses the base project I own runs on our internal servers for logs that customers have voluntarily sent to us for diagnostic purposes. But we have another project that uses log parsers, bundles them into an executable, and has our customer run them with a rules engine as a diagnostic health check in their network.

    The method by which both of these projects are deployed is hugely different and causes a massive logical split between the two. But they both use the same data source, cluster logs. 

    What this did was cause both teams to create two sets of parsers which hugely overlapping work. When we looked at the cost to the company for time spent doubling our work it was a hefty penny that we want to avoid in the future.

So now I'm looking at different revision control systems or different tooling solutions for git.

The issue as I understand it is that the apache2ctl command we're running doesn't provide the same output in apache 2.2 as it does in 2.4 meaning that for a 2.2 system we won't see these Define statements.  I've been looking through apache documentation (without much success) to try to find how to get apache2ctl to spit these out on 2.2

The code as it's written now throws an error if Define: DUMP_RUN_CFG isn't output, thinking that the apache2ctl call has failed. Since 2.2 doesn't output anything except "Syntax OK" the client errors out. So we could for example not error out in this case and just tell 2.2 users that their mod_define statements aren't supported, though I'm not sure what effect this has. Presumably we could also make this version dependent so it wouldn't effect the case for v2.4 users.

UPDATE:
The current behavior actually does allow loading non-encrypted keys. This can be achieved by passing an empty string as password, which does not trigger the prompt, but skips decryption.

IMHO, this behavior is not obvious. I think it is better to fail if an empty password is passed. If a caller wants to use the function to load a non-encrypted key, s/he should just not pass a password.

However, a user, who is prompted for a password, can only enter either a non-empty or an empty password. To give that user the option to load a non-encrypted key, we should skip decryption if the user does not enter a password, i.e. the password is an empty string.

> This is the one that I am least comfortable with and will need to think it through. The concept of "1 week from x" is already well-captured by relativedelta and timedelta. Having a special class that explicitly ties that to "now" seems strange. Why is "time relative to the current time" an especially useful concept?

The goal with RelativeDate is to able to pass the idea of "1 week ago" as a minimum boundary to something like Marshmallow or DRF serializers without needing to implement custom validators. E.g. You can do something like:

```python
UTC_ONE_WEEK_AGO = RelativeDateTime(offset=timedelta(days=7)

class SomeSerializer(Schema):
    when = Datetime(validators=lambda d: d <= UTC_ONE_WEEK_AGO)
```

Rather than needing to type out: `lambda d: d <= (datetime.utcnow() - timedelta(days=7))` every time.

def interruptible_get_result(future):
    """
    Waits for the result of a future in a way that can be interrupted
    by a KeyboardInterrupt.

    This is not necessary in Python 3, but is needed for Python 2.
    """
    while True:
        try:
            return future.result(timeout=1.0)
        except futures.TimeoutError:

Note however that those finalizers have second purpose: The Context object needs to know when all other Z3Objects created from that context have disappeared, because only then is it safe to delete the context (See m_`ctx.m_refCount--;` in `Z3Object::dispose()`). Once `m_ctx.refCount` is down to 0, the API will call `Native.delContext(m_ctx)`, which is done in `Context::finalize()`. The current implementation is only sound for non-concurrent GCs, because I couldn't figure out how to re-queue something for finalization in Java, so this might need a bit more work.

> Is it correct to say that this implementation does not support sub-sampling the output in addition to dilating the weights?

Yes, this implementation does not support strides in addition to dilation, because it uses one of the convolution backward passes. That's a possible disadvantage, but I haven't seen strided dilated convolution in a paper yet. On the other hand, we're the only ones using cuDNN for dilated convolution now :)

>  Or would these options always cancel out, so it would not make sense?

No, in theory we could have strided dilated convolution. The easiest solution will be extending im2col and col2im in GpuCorrMM and CorrMM: https://github.com/torch/nn/pull/797/commits/7e86ba4f2b687d8cfe1d34fc8770506610bb4f94#diff-9c7194a6647abb997cb04d4e37a864f1L6
This *could* even be done conditionally, so we compile a different version of the kernel for dilated convolution, to avoid the extra multiplications if they're unneeded (not sure how much of a difference in performance this would make).

Another reason for integrating dilation into im2col/col2im would be that the current implementation doesn't support padding, as explained in the PR description.

So, first thing first what is HDOP. HDOP shows how good the geometry of the satellites is over the GPS receiver so as to be able to provide a unique solution to the problem of determining the GPS time as well as the (x,y,z) coordinates of the receiver. If the geometry is not good (satellites are collinear or concyclic with the receiver) then you get high HDOP values. 

Further,  representing a circle of precision would require us to estimate the UERE which I think is provided in the hacc value of one of the UBlox messages. So, maybe using the hacc you can draw a circle of precision.

Just adding my comments again from #1279 (see below).

There are potential problems introduced in expecting ganga to grab a pre-built target of an application from cvmfs that it was felt could simply be avoided by requiring a user actually build their own target to submit with their jobs.

The only way around this is to give ganga the ability to look for a pre-built target on a worker node (in the CVMFS mount) for an application and then to un-tar it to the WN directory.

This introcudes problems including what to do if the target ever needs re-building for a historical release due to a change in the `lb-*` applications.

With this in mind it was felt the best solution is to require a user always build a target before submitting a job.

The much better apporach (imo) is to do something like:

(pseudo-code):
```
myApp = prepareGaudiExec(...)
myApp.prepare()
box.add(myApp)
j=Job(...)
j.application = box[..]
j.application.extraOpts = ...
j.submit()
```

The above approach has the advantage that you only ever pre-build the application target once if needed.

The target is then stored (pre-built) in your cmtconfig space and you only ever need to re-compile if there is some change elsewhere in the LHCb stack.

Due to ganga's inheritance model changes made to j.application __do not__ impact the  stored myApp or object in the `box` repo.

This doesn't require the release process for LHCb apps to change or for historical releases on CVMFS to have to be touched. (both of these are extremely good things).



> My original comment from #1279
```
Hi again,

OK the job submission broadly has the following constraints:

Ganga doesn't know what (if anything) has been changed in a project.
i.e. Ganga has no way of querying does this LHCb application need to be rebuilt each time.
How should Ganga construct the environment for the job on the worker node?
i.e. How if something has changed should this overload the default components on the worker node.

These 2 problems are solved by the new LHCb build system having a Ganga build target.
This builds all of the required files needed to run a job into 1 .tar.gz target file.

This allows Ganga to capture this build-target for an application, arrange for it to be transferred to the worker node, then it additionally provides a consistent entry point which handles all of the environment initialisation within the LHCb build system.
This has the additional advantage that running on the scripts out of the Ganga build target is executing the same commands as you would when running locally, so less differences between Local/Batch/Grid running.

In the case that a user just wants to run a vanilla release of DaVinci with different options to production this is a special case.
Ganga (GaudiExec) still requires for the Ganga build target to exist for the vanilla build target. This makes life difficult as either there has to be a pre-built target distributed by CVMFS which Ganga can use or Ganga has to wrap up the default application build-area you get from an lb-dev command.
Distributing a pre-built target on CVMFS unfortunately has several disadvantages which includes a lot of code to look for a specific build-target, and, if there is a bug in the release would require some horrible hacks to work around it. (you can't (well really, should NEVER) change a file on CVMFS)
It was decided after some discussion it is much easier to request users to build a simple target once for submitting default DaVinci applications as this allows for fixes from the LHCb lb-scripts to be rolled out into production rather than requiring a re-release of all of the LHCb apps effected.
The situation may have changed, and if so it might be sensible to re-visit but I think the original ideas pushing for this model of working are better.

There is a question about whether the LHCb documentation should teach people how to use the tools or how they work and I'm strongly leaning toward the documentation being re-written with more of a focus on the latter.
```

I actually implemented a workaround for this. It involves overriding `load_modules` method on the `Dashboard` object. It now calls a new method called `get_or_create_module_models` every time which either gets that specific module from the DB if it exists, otherwise it creates it. This allows you to modify the Dashboard and it will update as you would expect it to.

```python
from jet.dashboard.dashboard import Dashboard
from jet.dashboard.models import UserDashboardModule


class CustomIndexDashboard(Dashboard):
    columns = 3
    # Moderator Tools

    def init_with_context(self, context):
        self.available_children.append(modules.LinkList)

    def get_or_create_module_models(self, user):
        module_models = []

        i = 0

        for module in self.children:
            column = module.column if module.column is not None else i % self.columns
            order = module.order if module.order is not None else int(i / self.columns)

            obj, created = UserDashboardModule.objects.get_or_create(
                title=module.title,
                app_label=self.app_label,
                user=user.pk,
                module=module.fullname(),
                column=column,
                order=order,
                settings=module.dump_settings(),
                children=module.dump_children()
            )
            module_models.append(obj)
            i += 1

        return module_models

    def load_modules(self):
        module_models = self.get_or_create_module_models(self.context['request'].user)

        loaded_modules = []

        for module_model in module_models:
            module_cls = module_model.load_module()
            if module_cls is not None:
                module = module_cls(model=module_model, context=self.context)
                loaded_modules.append(module)

        self.modules = loaded_modules
```

The Vision Processor D4 is designed to perform processes such as rectification to reduce the amount of calculations that the computing host-processor has to do.  The results of those calculations are then output over USB.  Some form of computing board should be required though to do something useful with that data output.

You would not necessarily have to connect the camera to a full PC.  System integrators could incorporate small 'single board' computers such as the Up Board and connect the camera to that.  Or if computing power closer to a desktop PC is needed in a small form-factor, an Intel NUC mini PC could be used.  

If you would like to explore this possibility, bundle deals for the 400 Series cameras and Up Board or NUC are both available in the RealSense section of Intel's online click store.

https://click.intel.com/realsense.html

Here's a specification sheet for the D4.

@peterlacko That would work. But all of the solutions proposed so far (including my own propositions!) have bothered me. What I'm aiming for is an orthogonal solution. As things stand now, you can place configuration files in a system-wide location (`/etc/xdg`), a per-user location (`~/.config`) and in a custom location, and these three options serve three different use cases. In addition, users can use a variety of test runners, including unittest2, nose, nose2 and py.test. I'd like to be able to serve all of these use cases with a single common solution.

If I allow the user to specify an absolute path to a configuration file, I'm making life harder to folks who are taking advantage of the XDG standard. And if I use the `--config` approach as in my first comment, then I'm shutting out users who'd like to use an alternate test runner.

I think a good solution is to let the user specify a file name via an environment variable, like so:

```sh
$ vi ~/.config/pulp_smash/settings2.json
$ PULP_SMASH_CONFIG_FILE=settings2.json \
> python -m unittest2 discover pulp_smash.tests
```

This is technically simple to accomplish, reasonably simple in practice, respects users who take advantage of the XDG standard to place their configuration files in unexpected and weird places, and works no matter which test runner is used.
